For the past decade, NASCAR Cup Series races have been relatively easy to
find: Fox carried the first half of the season, NBC carried the second. But=
 a couple of years ago, as NASCAR executives started negotiating new media =
rights, they discovered what other sports leagues had already figured out=
=E2=80=94spreading your schedule across as many platforms as possible was t=
he surest way to increase value.

=C2=A0

NASCAR=E2=80=99s new, much sweeter rights deals kicked off with last weeken=
d=E2=80=99s Daytona 500.
The seven-year pacts are worth a total of $1.1 billion annually, up from ab=
out $800 million, and now involve five media companies. Fox still carries t=
he opening races, NBC still carries the later ones. In between, Amazon Prim=
e and Warner Bros. Discovery will carry five races apiece. Adding further d=
iffusion, NASCAR sold the rights to its second-tier Xfinity Series to a fif=
th media company: Nexstar=E2=80=99s The CW. Months ago, in
Dude, Where=E2=80=99s My Nascar ( https://puck.news/nascar-americas-own-mot=
orsports-is-branching-out/?1740094945&utm_campaign=3DThe+Varsity+-+SUBSCRIB=
ERS+%282%2F20%2F25%29&utm_content=3DThe+Varsity+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F20%2F2=
5%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617=
da42abd12a ) (Part I), president Steve Phelps defended this paradigm by poi=
nting to progenitors in other sports.

=C2=A0

Predictably, the grumbling started as soon as these deals saw daylight, dra=
wing the same complaints soccer and tennis
and baseball fans have been making for years: it=E2=80=99s become too expen=
sive to sign up for all the services that carry a sport, and too difficult =
to figure out where to find a given event. In fact, just about every major =
sport is carried on a wide array of channels, streamers, and bundle variati=
ons these days.

=C2=A0

This won=E2=80=99t last forever, of course. Media executives often talk abo=
ut the =E2=80=9Cgreat rebundling,=E2=80=9D when several of the
streaming services merge, offering fans fewer options to find their games, =
but also fewer vendors to pay to see them. But while these complaints have =
grown louder as sports migrate to new platforms, there=E2=80=99s some evide=
nce that hardcore fans are following their favorite sports to different pla=
tforms. Paramount and NBC executives, for example, consistently say their b=
iggest subscriber boosts come from NFL and international soccer games, sugg=
esting that sports fan completists are still happy to pay
to never miss a game or match. Unfortunately, not everyone can be the NFL o=
r the Premier League.

-------------

NASCAR executives were conscious of these complaints as they negotiated the=
 new deals.
But they were also confident that the sport=E2=80=99s fervent fan base woul=
d follow the races wherever they happened to wind up, mostly because they=
=E2=80=99d done it before. Their old deals with Fox and NBC had included ra=
ces airing on channels like FS1 and USA. =E2=80=9COur fan base was accustom=
ed to some channel hopping,=E2=80=9D said Brian Herbst, NASCAR=E2=80=99s C.=
R.O. and executive vice president of media, echoing Phelps=E2=80=99 comment=
s to me months ago. The league believes that the races will be even easier =
to find
this time around because they follow a set schedule, even if they appear on=
 different networks: Thursdays are for Duel races, Fridays for Truck races,=
 Saturdays for Xfinity races, and Sundays for Cup races.=C2=A0

The BMW 7 Series pairs undeniable luxury with high performance. Learn more =
at
BMWUSA.com ( http://bmwusa.com?utm_campaign=3DThe+Varsity+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%28=
2%2F20%2F25%29&utm_content=3DThe+Varsity+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F20%2F25%29&ut=
m_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd=
12a ).

Unlike the NFL, which may have a Monday night game on ESPN and maybe a game
the following Wednesday on Netflix and then another Thursday one on Amazon,=
 NASCAR has more week-to-week continuity and fewer switches. =E2=80=9CIt's =
easier for us to say, =E2=80=98You=E2=80=99re going from the Fox portion of=
 the season into the Amazon and the TNT portion, and then the NBC portion,=
=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D said Herbst. =E2=80=9CWe have an advantage in terms of s=
etting up the weekend, one weekend after the next, from February to Novembe=
r, versus trying to understand where a game may be based on the partner kin=
d of jumping around
different channels or digital platforms.=E2=80=9D

=C2=A0

Of course, NASCAR also has loads of promotional and marketing campaigns des=
igned to let fans know where to find the races. And the mediacos will promo=
te each others=E2=80=99 races, similar to the way the networks treat the NF=
L and Big Ten and NBA Playoffs. =E2=80=9CWhen you see Amazon get closer to =
their launch window, which is Memorial Day, you=E2=80=99ll see a heavy mark=
eting push and
promotional campaign from Amazon in April and May, which will include third=
-party spend,=E2=80=9D said Herbst. That=E2=80=99s similar to The CW=E2=80=
=99s approach during Daytona, when it pushed a tune-in marketing campaign a=
cross its 200-plus affiliates. =E2=80=9CThey certainly spent to make sure t=
hat our fan base and our viewers were conditioned to the fact that the Xfin=
ity series will be on CW,=E2=80=9D Herbst said. Perhaps it=E2=80=99s no sur=
prise, then, that the 1.8 million viewers who tuned into The CW=E2=80=99s f=
irst race last weekend comprised
the Xfinity series=E2=80=99 largest audience in two years.

=C2=A0

Interestingly, the 6.8 million viewers who tuned into Fox=E2=80=99s coverag=
e of Daytona bested both the NBA All-Star Game and the NHL=E2=80=99s 4 Nati=
ons Face-Off. It was NASCAR=E2=80=99s biggest audience in two years. And if=
 anyone still needs reminding, the fan base is broader than you think. When=
 Phelps was on the Varsity podcast back in November, he said he
expected ( https://puck.news/podcast_episode/nascars-next-gear-with-steve-p=
helps/?utm_campaign=3DThe+Varsity+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F20%2F25%29&utm_conte=
nt=3DThe+Varsity+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F20%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&=
utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) that the number of=
 NASCAR fans who subscribe to Amazon Prime would grow considerably as it be=
gan carrying races. =E2=80=9CThat universe is, right now, actually higher i=
n terms of a subscriber base than the cable universe,=E2=80=9D he said. =E2=
=80=9CDo I think our fans will find it? I am confident that they will.=E2=
=80=9D

=E2=80=8A

From the Cheap Seats
--------------------

On the Varsity pod with Alex Sherman: =E2=80=9CYou brought up a great point
about YouTube being a sleeping giant for NFL rights. I think many in the sp=
orts media have just gifted [the NFL to Netflix] incrementality, but Google=
 has scale and money, too. With the NFL having seven international games in=
 2025, it would appear there=E2=80=99s enough to go around to satisfy the P=
eacock and ESPN+ streaming exclusive games, four NFL Network games, leaving=
 one game that could be available to a YouTube or Netflix as a test before =
the NFL=E2=80=99s next round of bids. I had always considered
Apple a sleeping giant, given the Apple Music relationship, but the scale d=
iscussion you had with Alex has walked me off of that ledge.=E2=80=9D =E2=
=80=94A Varsity subscriber

=C2=A0

On placing Grant Hill on the Mount Rushmore of former NBA players who went =
on to succeed in business alongside Shaq, Magic, Junior Bridgeman, and Mich=
ael Jordan: =E2=80=9CNaming a five-person Mount Rushmore is surely the writ=
ers=E2=80=99
equivalent of a modern All-Star event: extra flash to cover the intentional=
 lack of effort, so no one gets hurt.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94A Varsity subscribe=
r

=C2=A0

[Ed. note: It was Marchand=E2=80=99s idea!]

=C2=A0

On NASCAR ratings: The CW ratings increase last season was for the second-t=
ier
Xfinity Series (which is now exclusively on CW). I=E2=80=99m curious to see=
 how the Cup ratings fare with so many fewer races on broadcast TV (just ei=
ght points races on Fox and NBC, compared to 20 last season). =E2=80=94A lo=
cal-news executive producer

=E2=80=8A

Have a great weekend. See you Monday,

John

50%;text-align:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=3D"margin:0;margi=
n-bottom:0px;">For the past decade, NASCAR Cup Series races have been relat=
ively easy to
find: Fox carried the first half of the season, NBC carried the second. But=
 a couple of years ago, as NASCAR executives started negotiating new media =
rights, they discovered what other sports leagues had already figured out=
=E2=80=94spreading your schedule across as many platforms as possible was t=
he surest way to increase value.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;=
">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">NASCAR=E2=80=99s new, =
much sweeter rights deals kicked off with last weekend=E2=80=99s Daytona 50=
0.
The seven-year pacts are worth a total of $1.1 billion annually, up from ab=
out $800 million, and now involve <em>five </em>media companies. Fox still =
carries the opening races, NBC still carries the later ones. In between, Am=
azon Prime and Warner Bros. Discovery will carry five races apiece. Adding =
further diffusion, NASCAR sold the rights to its second-tier Xfinity Series=
 to a <em>fifth </em>media company: Nexstar=E2=80=99s The CW. Months ago, i=
n
<a href=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2Uktx=

I), president <strong>Steve Phelps </strong>defended this paradigm by point=
ing to progenitors in other sports.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0=
px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Predictably, the gr=
umbling started as soon as these deals saw daylight, drawing the same compl=
aints soccer and tennis
and baseball fans have been making for years: it=E2=80=99s become too expen=
sive to sign up for all the services that carry a sport, and too difficult =
to figure out where to find a given event. In fact, just about every major =
sport is carried on a wide array of channels, streamers, and bundle variati=
ons these days.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p st=
yle=3D"margin:0;">This won=E2=80=99t last forever, of course. Media executi=
ves often talk about the =E2=80=9Cgreat rebundling,=E2=80=9D when several o=
f the
streaming services merge, offering fans fewer options to find their games, =
but also fewer vendors to pay to see them. But while these complaints have =
grown louder as sports migrate to new platforms, there=E2=80=99s some evide=
nce that hardcore fans are following their favorite sports to different pla=
tforms. Paramount and NBC executives, for example, consistently say their b=
iggest subscriber boosts come from NFL and international soccer games, sugg=
esting that sports fan completists are still happy to pay
to never miss a game or match. Unfortunately, not everyone can be the NFL o=
r the Premier League.</p></div>
